## [24:22 - 27:33]

Tell your client a personal story from your life or work with another person. Remember, whenever you talk about another person, your client is thinking about themselves. But we automatically refer to our own life when we hear someone else's example. So you can also use folk tales and myths about a quest. Your listener will naturally relate themselves into the role of the character on the quest. I heard a wonderful example of this. This was in an example of hypnosis that I found online that a young man was teaching. I don't remember his name. I'm sorry, I wish I could give him credit for this. But he was talking about meeting with a friend who had a problem that he was very stuck in his own life. He couldn't make decisions. And so the man presenting the story, the coach, he said to his friend in a casual way, you know, I haven't told you about my trip to Asia. What was amazing when I was there is that whatever country I was in, I didn't know anything. I didn't know the language. I would get lost. I would actually get into quite dangerous situations. But what always happened was I found a solution. I always found it a way out and I went to the next country and I continued my trip. And I look back on it and think what an incredible trip it was that time I spent in Asia. ## [33:39 - 36:42]

Michael Gasaniga, who has become very popular because of the book titled Who's in Charge, where he proposes that even in spite of other research on the brain, the mind is still in charge. If you've read the book, you will probably remember this story. This is a physical metaphor that when overcome can change the life of the person. So this was a girl just to give you a brief version of the story. I think she was in college and she was answering a question at the front of the class when she lost control and farted very loudly. She went to Erickson and said that she was so ashamed she would never go back to school and she didn't want to leave her home. I'm paraphrasing. I have not read the story for a few months. I'm just, this is what my memory remembers.

## [42:55 - 45:56]

So Erickson speaks to her about the wonders of the human body and how it can keep in and let out different things. Air, liquid, solid matter. And the woman's feelings about her body and its natural functions, they could have been turned into a whole other type of therapy, right? But Erickson stays within that physical metaphor and he instructs her to eat beans and learn to fart naturally in all different ways. And through doing that, I think she's instructed to do this at home alone and sometimes naked, sometimes with clothes on, and to learn about all these different ways that she can fart. And he allows her to, or sorry, he also helps her create a future where she becomes more comfortable with herself. And he shares in his tale how he visits her in the future, years after their time together. And she's just had a baby and she nurses the baby openly in front of him. So as a listener to this story, we understand that not only did she become comfortable with her body, she found love and she started a family. As a listener, a person is always looking for how this tale relates to them, what will they gain? And they may not need to learn to fart, but they may want to find a partner or start a family. So this would be an interesting story to tell someone who we may feel is not very comfortable with themselves and that discomfort is preventing them from finding love. We might tell the story of the girl who farted in front of the classroom. Just as an example, Mark, can you move forward one side? So small changes are always possible and repeatedly Erickson teaches us that when a client makes a small change, it is the beginning of a bigger and ultimately transformative life change. Remember the girl who learns to stop herself from bedwetting. This starts with the realization that if a man opened the door on her in the bathroom, she would stop urinating. So when she tells Erickson that that would be her reaction, she has just realized that she has the control over her muscles to stop urination, which she thought was not possible for her due to a lot of medical interventions that she had. And her problem was that she was a bedwetter. So that's the beginning of his work with her is her realization that when afraid, she can stop herself from urinating. And that girl goes on to learn to completely control her bedwetting and have sleepovers with friends and a very normal life, which when she began working with him, she did not think was possible. Another great story in the book is the man who believed he didn't have a stomach.

## [45:56 - 48:57]

He was very, very thin, I think 80 pounds after being a man that weighed over 200 pounds. And one of the first things Erickson does is he feeds him. It's like after a clinic saying that he has no stomach, that's why he's getting... Right. He's getting fed through a tube. And they're feeding him, I think, 3,000 calories a day and he's not gaining any weight. So what are you thinking? Wait a moment. I'm confused. Did he actually have a stomach or not? Yes, he has a stomach, but he tells the doctors he does not have a stomach. And that's why he's lost so much weight and they tube feed him. And Erickson takes on this patient and he lowers the amount of calories first. And then he feeds the man one day after knowing him for a couple of weeks. He feeds him a lot of cream and seltzer and fish oil that will produce very disturbing burps in the man. And so when the man burps, he realizes that... Well, he's forced to realize that he has a stomach because one can't burp if they don't have a stomach. And so that's the first small change. But as you read the story, you learn that the man goes on to regain all the weight and become a normal person. By normal, I mean functioning. He's able to leave the hospital after years. He's able to work and he's able to reengage in his life. Another story was one of a young man who's a nail biter. And the parents bring him to therapy with Erickson, I believe primarily for nail biting, even though he's flunked out of medical school and he's also ruined his projectory as a concert pianist. It's an interesting story of a complicated family. But what I wanted to share with you about that story is that Erickson talks to the man about whether it would be possible to stop biting nine of his nails and only bite one. And the young man says, oh, Dr. Erickson, now I can no longer bite my nails, can I? Because you've proved to me that I could just be biting one of them. So that's an example of how that understanding of the person's own control over what they thought was an uncontrollable action is the beginning of how they change. They start to see what they're able to do just through the conversation with Erickson.

## [48:57 - 51:57]

So that's something that we all have the ability to do when we speak to someone. We can make something that seems very large, much more negotiable when we understand what the parts are, and when we help our client to understand what they can do. Yeah, I mean, one thing that's... Sorry. No, go ahead. I think one thing that's the takeaway for this lecture I think is really important is that the amount of... Erickson had tremendous patience for anyone he was working with. So, and we think one of my jobs is to remind people when we watch, for instance, a Tony video or an intervention that's on stage with people, and to give you a framework of how to translate that into your own practice as a strategic interventionist and a coach, is that we're used to seeing these kind of one-hour breakthrough-type sessions, but really for Erickson, he would be delighted if he gets one adjustment from a person that takes him along the road. So he will look at someone like that, the man who has a delusion that he has no stomach, and he will, in his mind, think, well, if I can get him to do this, then he's going to see this. And if I can do that, and he breaks it into, you know, 10 steps. And every day, he will meet with the person for a very short time and reinforce that step, but the power of those small steps, and they come bound to the point where, even though the man is still technically crazy in this case, he is able to function and do what he's used to doing and then live his life. So I think as coaches, it's important for us not to have that cultivate that incredible patience also for our clients, and realize sometimes people will need many small steps, and you can break down a problem down. And the nice thing is that even when a person changes something a very little bit, then they can sometimes make a leap, like the young man who had the nails, and he figured out he could control it even a little bit, and he'll sometimes make the intuitive leap and understand that the whole problem can be discarded. But for us as all those coaches, you don't expect, you don't push the person to have a breakthrough. ## [51:57 - 55:00]

There's a man who would only say hello and goodbye to someone. He would, they had to be taken from place to place in the hospital. He wasn't functional. He would just stand. And her husband was to go visit them on the weekends, and she would stay away from home until this new lifestyle had managed to return her to her normal weight. And the woman not only followed his instructions, but she spent a year traveling the country with her children. And I think it's just very important for us to help others and to help ourselves learn to use emotional reward in a very positive way in our own life so that we're really able to have the relationships and the love that we want and that we don't have to use methods that just give us attention.
